---
title: "Building R Packages"
author: "Julia Wrobel"
format:
  beamer:
    theme: metropolis
    aspectratio: 169
    fontsize: 14pt
    slide-level: 2
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: false
  warning: false
  message: false
header-includes:
  - \usepackage{xcolor}
  - \newcommand{\red}[1]{\textcolor{red}{#1}}
  - \newcommand{\green}[1]{\textcolor{green}{#1}}
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, eval = TRUE}
library(tidyverse)
```

## Overview

Today, we cover:

- Why build an R package?
- Package structure
- Development workflow with `devtools` and `usethis`
- Writing and documenting functions with `roxygen2`
- Managing dependencies
- Testing with `testthat`
- Checking and sharing your package

::: notes
Resources: Hadley Wickham's R Packages (2e), https://r-pkgs.org. This is free online and is the definitive reference.
:::

---

## Why build an R package?

R packages are the **fundamental unit of shareable, reusable, and testable R code**

- **Reuse**: stop copy-pasting functions across projects
- **Share**: others (and your future self) can install and use your code
- **Document**: forces you to write down what your functions do
- **Test**: formal testing infrastructure catches bugs early
- **Reproducibility**: version-controlled, installable code tied to a specific analysis

::: notes
Hadley: "Packages are the fundamental units of reproducible R code."
Even if you never share a package publicly, the discipline of structuring code as a package is valuable.
Many biostatistics methods papers now have companion R packages on CRAN or GitHub.
:::

---

## Why build an R package?

You already use packages every day

\vspace{5mm}

Building one is easier than you think - the tools have improved dramatically

```{r}
install.packages(c("devtools", "usethis", 
                   "roxygen2", "testthat"))
```

::: notes
devtools: umbrella package for package development workflow
usethis: automates repetitive setup tasks
roxygen2: in-source documentation
testthat: unit testing framework
:::

---

## Package structure

\fontsize{10pt}{11pt}\selectfont
At minimum, an R package is a directory containing:

\vspace{3mm}

```
mypackage/
├── DESCRIPTION       # metadata: name, version, dependencies
├── NAMESPACE         # what functions are exported/imported
└── R/                # all .R source files go here
```

\vspace{3mm}

Additional components you will typically add:

```
├── man/              # documentation (auto-generated by roxygen2)
├── tests/            # unit tests
├── vignettes/        # long-form documentation
└── data/             # package datasets
```

::: notes
You never need to create most of this by hand — usethis handles it.
NAMESPACE in particular is something you should almost never edit by hand.
:::

---

## Creating a package

`usethis::create_package()` sets up the full directory structure

```{r}
library(usethis)

create_package("~/mypackage")
```

This creates the skeleton and opens a new RStudio project.

\vspace{5mm}

Initialize version control immediately:

```{r}
use_git()
```

::: notes
Always initialize git right away. It makes it much easier to track what changes the various usethis functions make to your package.
create_package opens a new RStudio session in the package project.
:::

---

## The DESCRIPTION file

Metadata about your package. `create_package()` gives you a template:

\vspace{3mm}

\fontsize{10pt}{11pt}\selectfont
```
Package: mypackage
Title: What the Package Does (One Line, Title Case)
Version: 0.0.0.9000
Authors@R: person("Julia", "Wrobel", email = "julia.wrobel@emory.edu",
                   role = c("aut", "cre"))
Description: A longer description of what your package does.
License: MIT + file LICENSE
Encoding: UTF-8
Roxygen: list(markdown = TRUE)
RoxygenNote: 7.x.x
```

::: notes
Version numbering: major.minor.patch.dev (e.g., 0.0.0.9000 is a development version)
Roles: aut = author, cre = maintainer (must have exactly one cre)
License: MIT is standard for open source. use_mit_license() sets this up.
:::

---

## The DESCRIPTION file

Set up a license with `usethis`:

```{r}
use_mit_license()
```

\vspace{5mm}

Other common setup tasks:

```{r}
use_readme_rmd()    # adds a README
use_news_md()       # adds a NEWS.md changelog
```

::: notes
README is important for GitHub. It's what people see when they land on your repo.
NEWS.md tracks user-visible changes between versions.
:::

---

## Writing your first function

Create a new `.R` file in `R/` for each function (or group of related functions):

```{r}
use_r("rescale")
```

This opens `R/rescale.R`. Write your function:

```{r}
rescale01 <- function(x) {
  rng <- range(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  (x - rng[1]) / (rng[2] - rng[1])
}
```

::: notes
Convention: one function per file, file named after the function. For small related functions, grouping is fine.
Don't use library() or require() inside package functions — this is a common mistake.
:::

---

## The development workflow

\fontsize{10pt}{11pt}\selectfont
The key function is `devtools::load_all()`, which simulates installing and loading your package:

\vspace{5mm}

```
edit code in R/ → load_all() → test interactively → repeat
```

\vspace{5mm}

```{r}
library(devtools)

load_all()           # load all functions in R/
rescale01(c(0, 5, 10))
```

::: notes
load_all() is much faster than install + restart. Equivalent to Cmd+Shift+L in RStudio.
This is the core loop you will use constantly during development.
:::

---

## Documenting with roxygen2

Documentation lives **right above the function** as special comments starting with `#'`

\fontsize{10pt}{11pt}\selectfont
```{r}
#' Rescale a vector to [0, 1]
#'
#' @param x A numeric vector.
#' @return A numeric vector with values scaled to lie in \[0, 1\].
#' @examples
#' rescale01(c(0, 5, 10))
#' @export
rescale01 <- function(x) {
  rng <- range(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  (x - rng[1]) / (rng[2] - rng[1])
}
```

::: notes
@export is critical — without it, the function won't be visible to package users.
@examples is runnable and checked by R CMD check.
:::

---

## Documenting with roxygen2

Convert roxygen2 comments to `.Rd` help files and update `NAMESPACE`:

```{r}
document()
```

\vspace{5mm}

Now you can check the help page:

```{r}
?rescale01
```

::: notes
document() is equivalent to Cmd+Shift+D in RStudio.
It writes to man/rescale01.Rd and updates NAMESPACE automatically.
Never edit man/ or NAMESPACE by hand.
:::

---

## The NAMESPACE file

Controls what your package **exports** (visible to users) and **imports** (used internally from other packages)

\vspace{3mm}

After `document()`, NAMESPACE looks like:

```
# Generated by roxygen2: do not edit by hand
export(rescale01)
```

\vspace{3mm}

- `@export` in roxygen → `export()` in NAMESPACE
- Functions without `@export` are internal only

::: notes
NAMESPACE is one of the trickiest parts of package development to understand.
The main rule: only export what users need. Keep internal helpers unexported.
:::

---

## Managing dependencies

\fontsize{10pt}{11pt}\selectfont
If your function uses another package, **do not** use `library()` inside it

\vspace{5mm}


Instead, declare the dependency in DESCRIPTION:

```{r}
use_package("dplyr")           # adds to Imports
use_package("ggplot2", "Suggests")  # optional dependency
```

\vspace{5mm}

Then refer to functions using `::`:

```{r}
my_function <- function(df) {
  dplyr::filter(df, x > 0)
}
```

::: notes
Imports: packages that must be installed for your package to work.
Suggests: packages used only in examples, vignettes, or tests.
The :: approach is explicit and avoids NAMESPACE clashes.
:::

---

## Importing specific functions

\fontsize{10pt}{11pt}\selectfont
If you use a function many times, you can import it directly with `@importFrom` to avoid writing `::` every time:

\fontsize{10pt}{11pt}\selectfont
```{r}
#' My function
#'
#' @importFrom dplyr filter mutate
#' @export
my_function <- function(df) {
  df |> filter(x > 0) |> mutate(y = x^2)
}
```

\vspace{3mm}

After `document()`, NAMESPACE will contain:

```
importFrom(dplyr,filter)
importFrom(dplyr,mutate)
```

::: notes
@importFrom is more explicit and CRAN-friendly than importing the whole package.
For the pipe |> specifically: if using base R pipe (R 4.1+) no import needed. If using magrittr %>%, use use_pipe().
:::

---

## Unit testing

A **unit test** checks that a small, isolated piece of code (a function) produces the correct output for a given input

\vspace{5mm}

Why bother?

- You will change your code. Tests tell you immediately if you broke something.
- Forces you to think carefully about edge cases and expected behavior
- Acts as living documentation — tests show how a function is meant to be used
- Makes collaboration safer: others can refactor with confidence

::: notes
The classic situation: you fix a bug, and in doing so introduce a new one elsewhere. Without tests you may not notice for a long time.
The cost of writing tests is low. The cost of not having them when something breaks is high.
:::

---

## Unit testing

Without tests, your informal workflow is probably:

1. Write function
2. Try it on one example in the console
3. Move on and hope for the best

\vspace{5mm}

With tests, that example **lives in the codebase** and is re-run every time you call `check()`

::: notes
Many experienced developers say: if it's worth running manually in the console to check, it's worth turning into a formal test.
:::

---

## Testing with testthat

\fontsize{10pt}{11pt}\selectfont
Formal unit tests catch bugs when you change your code

\vspace{5mm}

Set up the testing infrastructure:

```{r}
use_testthat()
```

\vspace{5mm}

Create a test file for your function:

```{r}
use_test("rescale")   # creates tests/testthat/test-rescale.R
```

::: notes
testthat is the standard testing framework for R packages.
use_testthat() adds testthat to Suggests in DESCRIPTION and creates tests/testthat/.
:::

---

## Writing tests

\fontsize{10pt}{11pt}\selectfont
```{r}
test_that("rescale01 maps range to [0, 1]", {
  expect_equal(rescale01(c(0, 5, 10)), c(0, 0.5, 1))
  expect_equal(rescale01(c(1, 1, 1)), c(NaN, NaN, NaN))
})

test_that("rescale01 handles NAs", {
  x <- c(NA, 0, 10)
  result <- rescale01(x)
  expect_true(is.na(result[1]))
  expect_equal(result[2], 0)
  expect_equal(result[3], 1)
})
```

Run all tests:

```{r}
test()
```

::: notes
test_that takes a description and a code block.
Common expect_ functions: expect_equal, expect_true, expect_false, expect_error, expect_warning, expect_snapshot.
Good tests: check expected behavior, check edge cases, check that errors are thrown correctly.
:::

---

## Checking your package

\fontsize{10pt}{11pt}\selectfont
`devtools::check()` runs the full suite of `R CMD check` tests — the same checks CRAN runs:

```{r}
check()
```

\vspace{3mm}

Checks include:

- Documentation is complete and correct
- All examples run without errors
- All tests pass
- No missing dependencies
- Code style issues

\vspace{3mm}

Aim for **0 errors, 0 warnings, 0 notes** before sharing.

::: notes
check() is equivalent to Cmd+Shift+E in RStudio.
Errors must be fixed. Warnings should be fixed. Notes are advisory but worth addressing.
Running check() regularly (not just before submitting) is good practice.
:::

---

## Workflow summary

\vspace{3mm}

| Task | `devtools` function | Shortcut |
|------|--------------------|----|
| Load package interactively | `load_all()` | Cmd+Shift+L |
| Update documentation | `document()` | Cmd+Shift+D |
| Run tests | `test()` | Cmd+Shift+T |
| Full check | `check()` | Cmd+Shift+E |
| Install locally | `install()` | |

\vspace{3mm}

Core loop: **edit → `load_all()` → explore → repeat**

::: notes
Memorize load_all(), document(), test(), check(). These four functions cover 90% of day-to-day package development.
:::

---

## Sharing on GitHub

\fontsize{10pt}{11pt}\selectfont
```{r}
# in your package project
use_github()   # creates remote repo and pushes
```

\vspace{5mm}

Others can install directly from GitHub:

```{r}
devtools::install_github("username/mypackage")
```

\vspace{5mm}

For a public package, add a `pkgdown` site:

```{r}
use_pkgdown()
use_pkgdown_github_pages()
```

::: notes
pkgdown builds a documentation website from your roxygen docs and vignettes.
Many CRAN packages also have pkgdown sites — e.g., https://dplyr.tidyverse.org
:::

---

## Vignettes

Long-form documentation that shows how to use your package in a realistic workflow

```{r}
use_vignette("introduction")
```

Creates `vignettes/introduction.Rmd` — a standard R Markdown document

\vspace{5mm}

Build and preview:

```{r}
build_vignettes()
```

::: notes
Vignettes are optional but very helpful for users.
They're checked by R CMD check, so all code in them must run.
:::

---

## Summary of `usethis` functions

\fontsize{10pt}{11pt}\selectfont

| Task | Function |
|------|----------|
| Create package | `create_package()` |
| Initialize git | `use_git()` |
| Add license | `use_mit_license()` |
| Create GitHub repo | `use_github()` |
| Add a function file | `use_r("name")` |
| Add a test file | `use_test("name")` |
| Set up testthat | `use_testthat()` |
| Add a dependency | `use_package("pkg")` |
| Add a vignette | `use_vignette("name")` |
| Add README | `use_readme_rmd()` |

::: notes
usethis automates all the boilerplate. You rarely need to create files or edit DESCRIPTION by hand.
Full reference at https://usethis.r-lib.org.
:::

---

## RCPP


---



## About Rcpp

>"Sometimes R code just isn’t fast enough. You’ve used profiling to figure out where your bottlenecks are, and you’ve done everything you can in R, but your code still isn’t fast enough."  - Hadley Wickham


\vspace{3mm}

- Now we'll learn how to improve performance by rewriting key functions in C++
- Comes by way of the [Rcpp package](https://www.rcpp.org/)
  - Makes it simple to connect C++ code to R

---

## About Rcpp

Typical bottlenecks that C++ can address include:


- Recursive functions, or problems which involve calling functions millions of times. The overhead of calling a function in C++ is much lower than that in R.
- Problems that require advanced data structures and algorithms that R doesn’t provide. Through the standard template library (STL), C++ has efficient implementations of many important data structures, from ordered maps to double-ended queues.
- Loops that can’t be easily vectorised because subsequent iterations depend on previous ones.
- Linear algebra stuff

::: notes
We're just going to talk a bit about some of the simpler stuff you can do with Rcpp
:::

---

## A note on Rcpp

You don't really need to know C++ to use Rcpp.

- But... Rcpp is notoriously annoying to debug.


---

## Getting started with C++

`cppFunction()` allows you to write C++ functions in R:

```{r, eval = TRUE}
library(Rcpp)

cppFunction('int add(int x, int y, int z) {
  int sum = x + y + z;
  return sum;
}')

# add works like a regular R function
add(1, 2, 3)

```

::: notes
When you run this code, Rcpp will compile the C++ code and construct an R function that connects to the compiled C++ function. We’re going to use this simple interface to learn how to write C++.
- Talk about whats going on here
:::
---


## Basic syntax

Translate R functions to their C++ equivalents. Gonna go through some increasingly complex examples:

- No inputs and a scalar output
- Scalar input and scalar output
- Vector input and scalar output
- Vector input and vector output
- Matrix input and vector output

---


## No inputs, scalar output

This function has no arguments and always returns the integer 1:

```{r}
one <- function(){1L}
```

The equivalent C++ function is:

```{r, eval = FALSE}
int one() {
  return 1;
}
```

We can compile and use this from R with `cppFunction`:

```{r, eval = FALSE}
cppFunction('int one() {
  return 1;
}')
```


---


## No inputs, scalar output

Reveals important differences between C++ and R:

- The syntax to create a function looks like the syntax to call a function; you don’t use assignment to create functions as you do in R.
- You must declare the type of output the function returns (`int` for scalar integer).
- Scalars and vectors are different
  - Common types of vectors are: `NumericVector`, `IntegerVector`, `CharacterVector`, and `LogicalVector`  
  - The scalar equivalents of numeric, integer, character, and logical vectors are `double`, `int`, `String`, and `bool`
- You must use an explicit `return` statement to return a value from a function.
- Every statement is terminated by a ;

---

## Scalar input, scalar output

The next example function implements a scalar version of the `sign()` function which returns 1 if the input is positive, and -1 if it’s negative:

```{r, eval = FALSE}
signR <- function(x) {
  if (x > 0) {
    1
  } else if (x == 0) {
    0
  } else {
    -1
  }
}

cppFunction('int signC(int x) {
  if (x > 0) {
    return 1;
  } else if (x == 0) {
    return 0;
  } else {
    return -1;
  }
}')
```


::: notes
- We declare the type of each input in the same way we declare the type of the output. While this makes the code a little more verbose, it also makes it very obvious what type of input the function needs.
:::

---


## Vector input, scalar output

One big difference between R and C++ is that the cost of loops is much lower in C++. In R we would never use the following code. But in C++, loops have very little overhead, so it’s fine to use them. 

```{r, eval = TRUE}
sumR <- function(x) {
  total <- 0
  for (i in seq_along(x)) {
    total <- total + x[i]
  }
  total
}
```

```{r, eval = TRUE}
cppFunction('double sumC(NumericVector x) {
  int n = x.size();
  double total = 0;
  for(int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
    total += x[i];
  }
  return total;
}')
```



::: notes
The following R code is terrible because we would never do this in a loop, we would use vectorization. However, this shows you how it works in both.
- To find the length of the vector, we use the .size() method, which returns an integer. C++ methods are called with . 
:::

---

## Vector input, scalar output

```{r, eval = TRUE}
```


```{r, eval = TRUE}
x <- runif(1e3)
microbenchmark(
  sum(x),
  sumC(x),
  sumR(x)
)
```


```{r, eval = TRUE}
```

---

## Vector input, vector output

Next we’ll create a function that computes the Euclidean distance between a value and a vector of values:

```{r}
pdistR <- function(x, ys) {
  sqrt((x - ys) ^ 2)
}
```

```{r}
cppFunction('NumericVector pdistC(double x, NumericVector ys) {
  int n = ys.size();
  NumericVector out(n);

  for(int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
    out[i] = sqrt(pow(ys[i] - x, 2.0));
  }
  return out;
}')
```

::: notes
- We create a new numeric vector of length n with a constructor: NumericVector out(n).
:::

---


## Matrix input, vector output

Each vector type has a matrix equivalent: `NumericMatrix`, `IntegerMatrix`, `CharacterMatrix`, and `LogicalMatrix`. For example, we could create a function that reproduces `rowSums()`:

```{r, eval = TRUE}
cppFunction('NumericVector rowSumsC(NumericMatrix x) {
  int nrow = x.nrow(), ncol = x.ncol();
  NumericVector out(nrow);

  for (int i = 0; i < nrow; i++) {
    double total = 0;
    for (int j = 0; j < ncol; j++) {
      total += x(i, j);
    }
    out[i] = total;
  }
  return out;
}')
set.seed(1014)
x <- matrix(sample(100), 10)
rowSumsC(x)
```


::: notes
- In C++, you subset a matrix with (), not [].
- Use .nrow() and .ncol() methods to get the dimensions of a matrix.
:::

---

## Using sourceCpp

So far, we’ve used inline C++ with `cppFunction()`. For real problems, it’s usually easier to use stand-alone C++ files and then source them into R using `sourceCpp()`.

<br>
Your stand-alone C++ file should have extension `.cpp` and  start with:

```{cpp}
#include <Rcpp.h>
using namespace Rcpp;
```

And for each function that you want available within R, you need to prefix it with:

```{cpp}
// [[Rcpp::export]]
```

Note that the space is mandatory.

---

## Using sourceCpp

To compile the C++ code, use `sourceCpp("path/to/file.cpp")`.
- This will create the matching R functions and add them to your current session.

<br>
You can embed R code in special C++ comment blocks. This is really convenient if you want to run some test code:

```{cpp}
/*** R
# This is R code
*/
```

---


## Using sourceCpp

```{cpp}
#include <Rcpp.h>
using namespace Rcpp;

// [[Rcpp::export]]
double meanC(NumericVector x) {
  int n = x.size();
  double total = 0;

  for(int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
    total += x[i];
  }
  return total / n;
}

/*** R
library(microbenchmark)
x <- runif(1e5)
microbenchmark(
  mean(x),
  meanC(x)
)
*/
```


???
Save and run on the fly
---


## Rcpp sugar

Rcpp provides a lot of syntactic “sugar” to ensure that C++ functions work very similarly to their R equivalents. In fact, Rcpp sugar makes it possible to write efficient C++ code that looks almost identical to its R equivalent. If there’s a sugar version of the function you’re interested in, you should use it: it’ll be both expressive and well tested.

<br>
Sugar functions can be roughly broken down into

- arithmetic and logical operators
- logical summary functions
- vector views
- other useful functions
---

## Rcpp sugar

All the basic arithmetic and logical operators are vectorised: : +, *, -, /, pow, <, <=, >, >=, ==. For example a simpler version of our `pdistC()` function:

```{r}
pdistR <- function(x, ys) {
  sqrt((x - ys) ^ 2)
}
```


```{cpp}
#include <Rcpp.h>
using namespace Rcpp;

// [[Rcpp::export]]
NumericVector pdistC2(double x, NumericVector ys) {
  return sqrt(pow((x - ys), 2));
}
```

???
Won't be faster than our C loop version but is easier to read
---


## Other important C++ syntax

- In C++, vector indices start at 0! 
- Use = for assignment
- C++ provides operators that modify in-place: total += x[i] is equivalent to total = total + x[i]. Similar in-place operators are -=, *=, and /=.
- Weird things can happen if you have missing values in an R object and pass them to C++

<br>
I am glossing over a **ton** of stuff. Lucky, there is good documentation if you need to implement this yourself.

---

## Using Rcpp in a package

To add `Rcpp` to an existing package, you put your C++ files in the `src/` directory and modify/create the following configuration files:

- In `DESCRIPTION` add
```{markdown}
LinkingTo: Rcpp
Imports: Rcpp
```


For more details see the Rcpp package vignette, `vignette("Rcpp-package")`.


---

## Final thoughts on converting R code to C++

- Test everything!!
  - Check that the output of your code in C++ matches the output of your code in R
- `RcppArmadillo` is a package with nice linear algebra syntax
  - Makes it easier to implement C++ code that uses a lot of matrix operations

- ChatGPT probably makes this a lot easier... but still test your code

---

## Package development demo

Next we're gonna make an R package that processes data from a pupillometer device.. this uses a real pipeline that I'm working on with Sarah Bird, a PhD student at Colorado.
- GitHub repo we're gonna turn into a package is at https://github.com/sarahmbird/pupillometer_pipeline



---

## Package development demo

```{r echo=FALSE, eval = TRUE, fig.align='center', out.width='120%'}
knitr::include_graphics("pupil.png")
```


---

## Using use_this()

Converting my repo to a package

- Does name of repo reflect name of package?
- Add imports.R file
- Make sure all R functions are in a folder called `R`

---

## Using use_this()

```{r}
library(usethis)
create_package()
use_mit_license()
use_git()
use_test()
use_roxygen_md()
use_package("stats")
use_vignette("pipeline", "Example pipeline")
usethis::use_data(DATAOBJECT, compress = "xz")
```


---

## My package development workflow

- load `devtools()`
- `load_all()`
- `document()`
- `check()`
- `install()`

---
## References

- Wickham H, Bryan J. [*R Packages* (2e).](https://r-pkgs.org)
- Wickham H. [*Advanced R* (2e), Chapter on R's package system.](https://adv-r.hadley.nz)
- [`usethis` documentation](https://usethis.r-lib.org)
- [`devtools` documentation](https://devtools.r-lib.org)
